text.title: Customer Success Basics — Agent Rules
text.summary: Safety rules for CS agents: re-engagement caps, billing autonomy prohibition, sentiment brittleness, PII handling.

rule.statement: Never let an agent issue refunds, plan downgrades, comp credits, or any billing action autonomously. Human approval is required at every billing touchpoint.
rule.rationale: Autonomous billing actions are irreversible, create accounting inconsistencies, and may violate subscription terms or payment processor rules. The cost of a single incorrect autonomous credit exceeds any automation efficiency gain.

rule.statement: Agent-written upsell prompts must include the customer's current plan in context before generating the prompt. Never offer a plan the customer is already on.
rule.rationale: An upsell email offering the customer's existing plan reads as broken and damages trust. This is a common failure when agent context does not include current subscription state.

rule.statement: Auto-generated case studies require explicit customer approval before publication. Never publish without a recorded approval step in the workflow.
rule.rationale: Publishing a customer's name, metrics, or story without consent creates privacy violations, potential NDA breaches, and reputational damage. Approval is non-negotiable.

rule.statement: When computing sentiment from support tickets or NPS, always include a confidence score. Route confidence below 0.7 to human review. Sarcasm, code-switching, and short responses make LLM sentiment brittle.
rule.rationale: A customer who writes "wow, great job" after a billing error is probably being sarcastic. LLMs score this as positive. False-positive health scores mask real churn risk.

rule.statement: Re-calibrate health-score thresholds after every significant product release. Rules built on pre-release behavior patterns fire incorrectly after the product changes.
rule.rationale: A health rule that fires when a customer has not used "feature X" becomes incorrect after feature X is removed or renamed. Stale rules produce wrong alerts and wrong suppressions.

rule.statement: Ground every automated outreach in a specific customer action: "you created project X 3 days ago and haven't returned" is personalized. "We noticed you haven't been active" is generic noise.
rule.rationale: Generic empathy trains customers to ignore automated messages. Specific behavioral grounding demonstrates that the system is paying attention and produces higher response rates.

rule.statement: Customer event logs contain PII (names, email, behavioral patterns, company data). Apply field-level redaction before sending to external LLM APIs. Aggregate summaries only through cloud endpoints.
rule.rationale: Product event logs often contain full user identification alongside sensitive behavioral data. Privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA) restrict sending this data to third-party processors without specific legal basis.

text.reference: Customer health score: usage (40) + adoption (30) + sentiment (20) + billing (10)
